CPC Central Committee, State Council congratulate Chinese Winter Olympics delegationThe Communist Party of China (#CPC) Central Committee and the State Council on Monday sent a congratulatory message to the #Chinese delegation for the Milan-Cortina 2026 Olympic Winter Games, commending the delegation for achieving its best-ever results in overseas #WinterOlympicGames.Winning five gold, four silver and six bronze medals, the delegation was praised for earning acclaim for the motherland and the Chinese people.The athletes demonstrated remarkable perseverance and sporting prowess, competed fairly and communicated friendly with their peers from around the world, once again showcasing China's image in the new era and inspiring patriotic passion and a spirit of hard work among Chinese people at home and abroad, the message read.The delegation was urged to focus on building China into a nation strong in sports, and to continue reaching for new heights in winter sports on the world stage, thus consolidating and expanding the country's achievement of "engaging 300 million people in winter sports."The Olympians were also encouraged to make new contributions to advancing the building of a great country and the national rejuvenation on all fronts through Chinese modernization.

23% of Super Bowl ads mentioned AI.That number landed differently depending on who watched.Creative professionals saw repetition. Marketing teams saw validation. Fans posting on social media saw another trend getting overplayed. "AI Bowl" became the nickname before halftime.The fatigue was real.But the volume wasn't random.These weren't product launches. Most ads didn't explain features or show use cases. They showed AI existing in daily routines. Ordering groceries. Answering questions. Managing schedules. The work wasn't in the selling. It was in the placement.Repeated exposure changes how something registers. First time feels new. Fifth time feels normal. Tenth time feels expected. By the time 23% of the night's ads reference the same technology, the question shifts from "do I need this" to "which version do I need."That's not speculation. That's how category normalization works in advertising. Make the concept familiar before the purchase decision arrives.Super Bowl ads cost millions per slot. Companies don't spend that to test creative concepts. They spend it to claim mental real estate when attention is concentrated. 23% percent of that real estate went to conditioning acceptance, not driving immediate action.The complaints about oversaturation are valid. But they confirm the strategy worked. We're debating execution quality instead of examining why this much budget went toward making AI feel unavoidable.Fatigue means the message landed.Where have you noticed AI showing up without asking for it?

Selective Outcomes Arbitrage on PolymarketThis strategy is used by less than 5% of traders but it brings 100% profit almost every time in the long runIt works on multi outcome marketsIn my case it's Super Bowl Champion 2026I buy YES from the top down until total cost gets close to around 0.90¢Rams - 16.3¢ → 0.163Seattle - 12.5¢ → 0.288Buffalo - 11.0¢ → 0.398Denver - 10.2¢ → 0.500Philadelphia - 9.0¢ → 0.590New England - 7.5¢ → 0.665San Francisco - 7.3¢ → 0.738Houston - 5.8¢ → 0.796Jacksonville - 5.8¢ → 0.854Chicago - 5.1¢ → 0.905I stop at Green Bay - the total portfolio is around 0.90 already and I want to leave about 10¢ upside on any hitIf any of these 10 teams take the Super Bowl, payout = 1$, net profit is around 9.9¢ or roughly 10% ROI///Across other multi outcome sports markets:CFB - in college football the title almost always goes to programs from the same elite pool that open the season near the top of the odds, so true underdog runs are rare and the selective top coverage gives less value for the riskNBA - even harder here, almost every champion came from a top 3 regular season seed, and low seeds only won the title a couple times in history, so the bulk YES buy down the table doesn’t really pay off long termSoccer (World Cup) - at World Cups and big tournaments the winner is almost always from the top 8 pre tourn odds, and teams from the bottom never reach the title, so multi outcome coverage there has poor EV///Why NFL - the league shows a rare combo. Champs mostly come from upper part of the preseason odds, but longshots pop off pretty often - like Raiders in 1981, 49ers in 1995, Rams in 1999 or Patriots in 200211 of the last 16 champs were underdogs in the final itself, which makes partial top coverage up to around 0.9$ especially attractive on risk reward basis///What do you think about this strategy, Polyfrens?Super Bowl Champion 2026 market: https://t.co/V82omWHS3H
